lessonTitle: Anti-Oppressive/Anti-Racist Practice and Mental Health Resources for Sickle Cell Disease

items.html: Identify elements of trauma informed care
Discuss stressors for people with Sickle Cell Disease, especially upon arrival to the Emergency Department
Identify available mental health resources in Ontario

data.html: Consider your social location and position as a healthcare provider. What are some ways that you see systems of privilege and oppression play out in healthcare on a day-to-day basis?

An approach that focuses on strengths as opposed to deficits:

  • People are resourceful and resilient
  • Strengths include knowledges, skills, coping strategies, relationships, values, hopes, and dreams
  • People develop strengths and resilience in the face adversity

Individuals and families can identify their own strengths. As healthcare workers, we can also support people with sickle cell disease and their families identify and amplify these strengths.

Examples of strengths in people with sickle cell disease, their families, and their communities:

  • Faith and Spirituality
  • Existing coping strategies
  • Worldview and outlook
  • Sense of purpose and values
  • Support networks and community roots
  • Past experiences and knowledge of solving problems

data.text: Resource: "Cosplaying to Cope" shows how one person with Sickle Cell Disease draws upon their unique skills and talents to manage their condition and live a fulfilling life.: https://www.rbcd.ca/blog/my-sickle-cell-disease-warrior-blood-moon-cosplaying-to-cope

data.html: Reflection Exercise: Think about a time where you have experienced a personal challenge. How did you get through it? What unique strengths were helpful? What did you learn from that experience?

slides.title: Let's look at what a trauma informed approach to care for patients in the ED, considering what might be running through a patient's mind

items.html: How am I gonna pay rent?
Do I have sick time?
I am going to my refugee hearing date
I have no health insurance coverage
I'm gonna fail my course
My group members are gonna think I am a flake
My teachers will think I am lying
How am I gonna get my shift covered?
My boss is going to fire me!
Who is gonna watch my kids?
Who is gonna pick up my kids from school?
How long will my kid be in hospital?
How am I gonna look after everyone else?
They are gonna think I am a drug seeker
They won't believe my pain is real
I'm going to get bad care

data.html: Now that we have gone over the mental health challenges and strategies for wellness, let's review what Mental Health resources are available to help you help your patient.

items.html:
24/7 Telephone Counselling
a. Suicide Crisis Help Line (Canada-Wide) Call 988 or visit www.988.ca
b. Kids Help Phone (Canada-wide for youth 29 and under) Call 1-800-668-6868 or Text 686868
Engage Primary Care
Provider 211 (Finding local community resources) Call 211 or visit www.211ontario.ca
ConnexOntario (Access to addiction, mental health, and problem gambling services) Call 1-866-5531-2600 or visit www.connexontario.ca
Bounce Back (Coaching support for mental health and wellbeing) Visit www.bouncebackontario.ca

Ontario Structured Psychotherapy (OHIP-funded psychotherapy from a cognitive behavioural therapy approach) Visit https://www.ontariohealth.ca/getting-health-care/mental-health-addictions/depression-anxiety-ontario-structured-psychotherapy
Hospital-Based Mental Health Programs
Employment-Based Mental Health Programs and Coverage

data.html: Write down one thing about how you will commit to practising trauma informed care. This can be a trauma-informed practice you hope to integrate into your work.

slides.title: Lived Experience Advocate: What Mental Health Resources Do You Use?

data.html:
Thea Campbell is a person with Sickle Cell Disease who wanted to ensure healthcare professionals incorporate mental health services into the care of their patients, by sharing how she utilizes these essential services.

Note: Thea refers to her disease as "being a carrier to Sickle Cell Anemia", but she does have Sickle Cell Disease and is not a Carrier who would have Sickle Cell Trait.

items.html: People with sickle cell disease and their families have developed strengths through their experiences of adversity.
Mental Health resources can be a maze to navigate - it's hard to find services, Don't not give up and seek help.

candidates.suggestion: The Strengths-Based Practice slide combines two reflection exercises and the main prose into one slide. If slide length is a concern, the second reflection exercise (Think about a time...) could be split onto its own slide.
candidates.reason: The second reflection exercise (line 194 in source) immediately follows the Strengths-Based Practice section prose and the collab image — merging keeps thematic unity, but the slide is dense.

candidates.suggestion: The ED stressors slide has 16 bullet thoughts plus 6 images. The 6 images could be split into two image_gallery blocks (3 each), interspersed between the bullet groups, if the gallery renders too dense.
candidates.reason: 6 images in a single gallery block may be visually crowded; splitting into two blocks with the content_list between them could improve readability.

candidates.suggestion: The 'Trauma-Informed Practices in Healthcare' slide title has no body content from the source beyond the transitional sentence. Consider whether EdApp had a hidden list or infographic here that was not captured in the Markdown.
candidates.reason: The source has only the bold header 'Trauma-Informed Practices in Healthcare' followed by a transitional sentence — no substantive body. This may have been an interactive EdApp screen with hidden content not exported to Markdown.
